serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mutton Jalfrezi is approximately 250 g, usually accompanied by rice or bread such as naan or roti.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Jalfrezi — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Jalfrezi (cr_id G00010930). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat-and-vegetable curry with low-GI vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Jalfrezi. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for mixed meat-and-vegetable curry dishes with predominantly low-GI vegetables (bell peppers, tomatoes, onions) and moderate protein content. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (35 × 18.6) / 100 ≈ 6.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100 g with insufficient dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) to offset borderline sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as the sodium content of 467 mg/100 g approaches the borderline threshold and the dietary fibre at 0.3 g/100 g is far below the 3 g/100 g level needed to provide meaningful cardiovascular offset; pairing with high-fibre sides such as lentils or whole-grain bread and ensuring generous vegetable portions to boost potassium intake is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (7.44 g/100 g) and sugar (4.69 g/100 g) with high protein (14.05 g/100 g) slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Jalfrezi has a low estimated glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content, making it a reasonable protein-rich option for people with Type 2 Diabetes when served with low-GI accompaniments such as basmati rice or whole-grain roti rather than white rice or white naan; portion size should be monitored as the moderate fat content (11.58 g/100 g) contributes to overall energy density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 85 mg/100 g, total fat 11.58 g/100 g, and sodium 467 mg/100 g from red meat (mutton/lamb)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mutton Jalfrezi is a red-meat dish with cholesterol at 85 mg/100 g and total fat at 11.58 g/100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (100 g or less), request reduced-oil and reduced-salt preparation, and consider substituting mutton with skinless chicken or fish to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol load, as red meat consumption is associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton (lamb) is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6); bell peppers, tomatoes, and chili are nightshade vegetables
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mutton Jalfrezi is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because mutton is a red meat high in arachidonic acid, a pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid that amplifies inflammatory pathways, and the dish also contains nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomatoes, chili) that may trigger individual RA flares; substituting mutton with chicken or oily fish and removing nightshades would substantially reduce the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Jalfrezi is a popular South Asian stir-fried curry dish made with tender pieces of mutton (lamb) cooked with colourful bell peppers, tomatoes, onions, green chili, ginger, and garlic in a spiced oil-based sauce. The name 'jalfrezi' derives from the Bengali/Urdu culinary tradition meaning 'hot-fry', reflecting the high-heat stir-fry technique used to develop a dry, semi-dry, or lightly sauced finish. The dish is seasoned with cumin, turmeric, coriander, and garam masala, giving it a bold, aromatic, and moderately spicy flavour profile with a slightly charred, savoury character. In Singapore, Mutton Jalfrezi is served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as a lunch or dinner main course, typically accompanied by basmati rice, naan, or roti prata. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, 7.44 g carbohydrate, 4.69 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, and 467 mg sodium. The high protein content from mutton supports satiety and muscle maintenance. However, the sodium level is elevated, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the red meat content and nightshade vegetables. The estimated glycemic index is low (~35), making it a reasonable choice for people managing Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI carbohydrates. Turmeric and ginger in the spice blend provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory and hypoglycaemic properties.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010930) with a canonical confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Mutton Jalfrezi is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010930) as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central does not carry an exact Mutton Jalfrezi entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB source record for this dish. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable mixed meat-and-vegetable curry dishes with low-GI vegetables; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Jalfrezi was found in the approved GI database. Halal status is inferred from the dish composition (mutton, vegetables, spices) assuming halal-certified preparation as is standard in Singapore Indian restaurants; kosher status is not confirmed. The dish description in the input refers to 'Tender Lamb pieces' — in Singapore culinary usage, 'mutton' and 'lamb' are used interchangeably for sheep meat; the canonical name Mutton Jalfrezi is retained per the HPB source.
